6: The Philistine priests' reference to Egypt: lāmmāh tᵉḵabbəḏû ʾet lᵉḇaḇḵem kaʾăšer kibbəḏû miṣrayim wəparʿōh ʾet libbām — 'Why do you harden your hearts (kāḇad = to make heavy) as Egypt and Pharaoh hardened their hearts?' The Exodus vocabulary is deliberately employed: the verb for 'harden' here is kāḇad (to be heavy), the same root as kāḇôḏ (glory). The Philistine priests have theological knowledge of the Exodus — a remarkable detail that positions the Ark Narrative as an extension of YHWH's sovereignty beyond Israel's borders. Pharaoh hardened his heart (kāḇad) and was destroyed; the Philistines are urged to learn from that precedent rather than repeat it. The same vocabulary of 'hardening' recurs in Mark 6:52 (the disciples' hardened hearts after the feeding) and Hebrews 3:8 (the warning not to harden hearts as in the wilderness).
19: YHWH strikes the men of Beth-shemesh for looking into the Ark — kî rāʾû bǎʾărôn YHWH, 'for they looked into the ark of YHWH.' Beth-shemesh (bêt šemeš, 'house of the sun') is a sun-cult city; the men treat the Ark with the same casual curiosity appropriate to a cult object that could be inspected. The holiness of the Ark is absolute — it cannot be looked into any more than YHWH's face can be seen directly (Exodus 33:20). The number struck (50,070 in the MT, probably a textual corruption) is less important than the theological point: proximity to divine holiness without covenant preparation is lethal. This is the theological ground of the NT's 'our God is a consuming fire' (Hebrews 12:29) and of the NT's emphasis on Christ as the mediator who makes approach to the holy God possible (Hebrews 10:19-22 — 'we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus').
1: Varon YHWH beshteh Pelishtim shivah chadashim (“the ark of the LORD was in Philistine territory for seven months”). The seven-month figure establishes the duration of the ordeal. Seven is the standard period of completeness/fullness in the Hebrew Bible (seven days, seven years, etc.). The ark’s seven-month captivity is a period of divine demonstration — sufficient to establish unambiguously that YHWH’s power operates independently of Israel’s military fortunes. The date formula (seven months) also allows the narrative to move efficiently from the disaster to the return without detailed chronological tracking.
2: The Philistines called their priests and diviners (kohanav ve-qosemav). Qosem (diviner) is from qasam (to divine/practice divination) — condemned in Israel (Deuteronomy 18:10, 14) but standard for Philistine religious specialists. The priests (kohanim) and diviners represent religious expertise; they are consulted because the ark’s return requires ritual precision. The question posed: mah na’aseh l’aron YHWH, hodi’enu bameh neshalchennu limqomo (“tell us how we should send it back to its place”). The request for instruction (hodi’enu, hiphil imperative of yada’) frames the consultation as technical religious inquiry.
3: The priests’ counsel: do not send it empty-handed (al-tishlechu oto reqam) but with a guilt offering (asham). The asham in Mosaic law is the reparation offering for inadvertent trespass against holy things (Leviticus 5:14-6:7): it involves restitution plus 20% to the wronged party. Here Philistine priests apply an analogous logic: an asham must accompany the ark because YHWH has been wronged by its capture and detention. Az yerapheh lakhem (“then you will be healed”) promises healing upon proper reparation. Venodah lakhem lammah lo-tasur yado mikem (“you will understand why his hand has not turned away from you”) — the reparation offering will clarify the causal relationship between offense and punishment.
4: Five golden tumors (ophalei zahav) and five golden mice (akhbarei zahav) corresponding to the five Philistine lords and cities. The principle of symbolic equivalence underlies the offering: the images represent the specific afflictions so that the deity can “take back” or release the curse through its representative symbol. This is consistent with Mesopotamian ritual practice of creating effigies of afflictions (Akkadian maqlû and shurpu texts describe burning effigies to remove curses). The mice (akhbar) are typically identified as field mice or rats, associated with plague transmission in many ancient sources and filling in the plague-detail suppressed in the Hebrew text of 1 Samuel 5.
5: Va’asitem tzalmei ophalekhem ve-tzalmei akhbareikhem hamshichitim et-ha-aretz (“make images of your tumors and images of the mice that are ravaging your land”). The word tzelem (image/likeness) is the same word used in Genesis 1:26-27 for humanity as tzelem elohim (image of God) and for the condemned images of Numbers 33:52. Its use for plague-effigies is ironic: the Philistine ritual inverts the concept of tzelem from being-in-the-image-of to representing-a-plague-image. Vetenu likvodr elohei Yisra’el (“give glory to the God of Israel”) — the Philistine priests counsel acknowledging YHWH’s kavod as the condition of release. Directly after the Ichabod-departure theology, the narrative shows YHWH’s kavod being acknowledged even by his enemies.
7: The test setup for the ark’s return: a new cart (agalah chadashah) and two nursing cows (parot alot asher lo-alah aleihen ol — “milk cows that have never been yoked”). The agalah chadashah (new/unused cart) echoes the requirement for dedicated sacred objects to be new and previously unconsecrated. The un-yoked cows are the ANE equivalent of an untouched/pure instrument: an animal that has never served any human purpose cannot have been ritually contaminated. The calves are to be taken away from the cows (ve-et banim hashevu me-achareihem ha-bayitah) — this is the critical test-condition, since cows would instinctively follow their calves rather than an unknown route.
8: The ark on the cart with the asham offerings in a box (aron) beside it. The ark and the guilt-offering box travel together — the offering accompanies the one to whom it is addressed. Veshalchtem oto vehalachu (“send it off and let it go”) — the double imperative/jussive form expresses the combination of human action (send it off) and resulting independent motion (let it go on its own). The test’s logic: if the cows go of their own accord to Israelite territory against their natural maternal instinct, that is a sign of divine direction.
9: The interpretive key: im derekh gvulah ya’aleh bet-shemesh (“if it goes up toward its own territory, to Beth-shemesh”) — then YHWH did this. If not: hayah lanu miqreh hu (“it happened to us by chance”). The word miqreh (chance/accident) appears in Ecclesiastes 2:14-15; 1 Samuel 3:19; 1 Samuel 9:2-3 as a philosophically charged term for events that happen to all without apparent cause. The Philistines deploy it as the counter-hypothesis to divine action: either YHWH did this, or it was random (miqreh). The narrative implicitly refutes the miqreh hypothesis: the cows go straight to Beth-shemesh (1 Samuel 6:12), mooing as they go (against natural behavior), without deviation.
10: Vaya’asu ha-anashim ken (“the men did so”). The report of compliance is terse — a single clause. The detailed instructions of 1 Samuel 6:7-9 are executed without comment. The narrative interest is in the result rather than the execution. Vayiqchu shetei farot alot — the noun parah (cow) used with alot (nursing/suckling) specifies lactating cows that would be under strong instinctive pull toward their confined calves.
11: The ark placed on the cart, the chest with the golden mice and golden tumor-images beside it. The itemization confirms the instructions were followed precisely: the narrative validates obedience. The two-item cargo — ark and asham-chest — corresponds to the two elements of the offering: the divine presence (ark) and the reparation offering (chest). The pairing mirrors the Levitical pattern of presenting both the object of worship and the accompanying offering.
12: Vayyisharnah ha-parot baderekh al derekh bet-shemesh (“the cows went straight in the direction of Beth-shemesh along one road”). The verb yashar (go straight, go directly) is a loaded term in Hebrew: ha-yashar be-einei YHWH (“what is right/straight in YHWH’s eyes”) is the covenantal standard of behavior. The cows behave with the moral and directional rectitude of creatures responding to YHWH’s guidance. They did not turn to the right or left (lo saru yamin u-smol) — the idiom for covenantal fidelity (Deuteronomy 5:32; Joshua 1:7). Veyigalu sarnei Pelishtim achareihem (“the Philistine lords followed them as far as the border”): the lords serve as witnesses to YHWH’s sign.
13: The Beth-shemeshites were reaping the wheat harvest (qotzerim qatzir-chittim ba-emeq) when they saw the ark. Qatzir chittim (wheat harvest) occurs in late spring (May-June), providing a seasonal marker. The sight of the ark prompted rejoicing (vayismechu lir’ot). Beth-shemesh was a Levitical city (Joshua 21:16), which explains both the presence of qualified handlers (Levites, 1 Samuel 6:15) and the enthusiastic reception.
14: The cart stopped (vataamod) at the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh. There was a large stone there (ve-sham aven gedolah); they split the wood of the cart (vayebaqe’u et atsei ha-agalah) and offered the cows as a burnt offering (olah laYHWH). The use of the cart wood and the cows as immediate offering material is an act of improvised sanctification: the materials that served as the sacred transport become the fuel and substance of the first offering upon return. The economy of consecration: nothing used in YHWH’s service is diverted to profane use.
15: The Levites took down the ark and the chest and set them on the large stone (al-ha-aven ha-gedolah). The large stone functions as a temporary altar. Vaya’alu olot vayizbechu zevachim bayyom hahu laYHWH (“offered burnt offerings and made sacrifices to the LORD that day”). The dual offering — olah (wholly burned, expressing total dedication) and zevach (shared meal offering) — marks the occasion as a full covenantal celebration of the ark’s return.
16: Va-yir’u chamishah sarnei Pelishtim vayyashubu Eqron bayyom hahu (“when the five lords of the Philistines saw it, they returned to Ekron that day”). The lords who followed as witnesses now depart as witnesses: having confirmed the sign (the cows went straight to Beth-shemesh), their role in the narrative is complete. Their return to Ekron closes the loop of 1 Samuel 5:10-6:16.
17: The enumeration of the five golden tumors: Ashdod, Gaza, Ashkelon, Gath, Ekron — the complete pentapolis. Ophalei zahav asher heshibu Pelishtim asham laYHWH (“the golden tumors that the Philistines returned as a guilt offering to the LORD”). The use of asham for the Philistine offering is the narrator’s validation of the Philistine priests’ ritual theology: what they called an asham functions as one. YHWH can receive acknowledgment of wrong even from those outside the covenant.
18: The golden mice corresponding to all Philistine cities — fortified cities and unwalled villages (me-ir mivtzar ve-ad kopher ha-parazi). The geographical completeness (fortified to unfortified, city to hamlet) parallels the theological comprehensiveness of the plague: no Philistine settlement was immune. Vaha’aven ha-gedolah asher hinichu aleha et-aron YHWH (“the large stone on which they set down the ark of the LORD is a witness to this day in the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh”). The etiological formula ad ha-yom hazeh (to this day) again anchors the narrative event in a physical landmark.
20: Mi yukhal la-amod lifnei YHWH ha-elohim ha-qadosh hazeh (“who is able to stand before the LORD, this holy God?”). The question is the Beth-shemeshite response to the deaths among them for looking into the ark. La-amod lifnei YHWH (to stand before YHWH) is covenant-service vocabulary: the priest who “stands before YHWH” (Deuteronomy 10:8; 1 Samuel 18:7) to minister. The deaths demonstrate that standing before YHWH’s holiness requires qualification. The theological question — who can stand before this holy God? — is ultimately answered by Psalms 24:3-4 (the one with clean hands and a pure heart) and definitively by Hebrews 4:14-16 (the great high priest who enables us to “approach the throne of grace with confidence”).
21: The Beth-shemeshites sent to Kiriath-jearim: heshibu Pelishtim et-aron YHWH (“the Philistines have returned the ark of the LORD”). Redu, ha’alu oto eleikhem (“come down and take it up to your place”). The directional verbs are geographically precise: Kiriath-jearim was in the Judean highlands above Beth-shemesh (going up = going to higher ground). The ark will remain at Kiriath-jearim for twenty years (1 Samuel 7:2) before David retrieves it (2 Sam 6). The ark’s long residence there, largely forgotten until David, represents the interval between the Ark Narrative’s conclusion and the Davidic era.
